5: seh tamim (śeh tāmîm): 'a lamb without blemish' — the Passover lamb must be tamim (perfect, without defect), the sacrificial standard that applies to all Levitical offerings (Leviticus 22:19-20) and is applied to Christ in 1 Peter 1:19: 'without blemish or spot.' The lamb is also ben shanah (in its first year), symbolically in the prime of life — not old and worn out. The NT applies the typology systematically: Christ is the Passover lamb (1 Corinthians 5:7), his bones were not broken per Exodus 12:46 (John 19:36), and his blood marks and protects the new covenant community.
1: vayomer YHWH el-Moshe veEl-Aharon beEretz Mitzrayim: 'YHWH said to Moses and Aaron in Egypt.' The address to both Moses and Aaron (rather than Moses alone relaying to Aaron) elevates the Passover instruction to the highest level of direct divine command. The phrase beEretz Mitzrayim ('in Egypt') is emphasized — this command is given while Israel is still in the land of bondage, before the exodus. The Passover ritual is thus defined as an act performed within the context of redemption-in-progress, not retrospectively from safety.
2: hachodesh hazeh lachem rosh chadashim: 'this month shall be for you the beginning of months.' YHWH resets the calendar: the month of the Passover (Nisan/Abib, spring) becomes the first month of Israel's sacred year. The calendar reset is a statement of sovereignty: YHWH defines time itself for his covenant people. The phrase lachem ('for you') is emphatic — this new calendar belongs to Israel specifically, as a testimony of their redemption. The year now begins with the Passover, encoding the memory of redemption into the very structure of time.
3: seh l'vet avot: 'a lamb/kid per household' (seh, H7716, small cattle — either sheep or goat, as Exodus 12:5 clarifies). The Passover is organized by households (beit avot, ancestral households) — not individuals, not the national assembly, but family units. The Passover is fundamentally a family meal, a household ceremony. This household structure is fundamental to the transmission of the covenant narrative to each generation (Exodus 12:26-27) and becomes foundational to the NT's household-church model (Acts 16:31-34: “believe in the Lord Jesus and you will be saved, you and your household”).
4: ufikku ish lefal ochlo: 'you shall count/divide up according to each person's eating.' The calculation principle (puk, to calculate, count) requires each household to estimate how many people can consume a whole lamb and share with neighbors if needed. The quantitative precision — every person counted, nothing wasted — reflects the covenantal seriousness of the Passover. Lifal ochlo (lit. 'according to the mouth of eating') uses the body-part metonymy peh (mouth) for eating. The communal sharing principle ensures no one is excluded for practical reasons.
6: ben haarbayim: 'between the two evenings' — a precise but debated temporal notation. The Pharisees understood it as between sunset (the first evening, when the sun goes down) and nightfall (the second evening, when darkness is complete) — roughly Exodus 3:00–5:00 PM. The Sadducees took it as between afternoon and sunset. The Synoptic Gospels place the Last Supper on the Passover night; John places the crucifixion on the day of Passover preparation (John 19:14) — Jesus dying as the lambs were being slaughtered. The timing of Christ's death aligns with the ben haarbayim slaughter of the Passover lamb.
7: velaqchu min-hadam venatenu al-shtei hamezuzot veal-hamashqof: 'they shall take some of the blood and put it on the two doorposts and the lintel.' The mezuzot (doorposts, from mezuzah, pillar/post) and mashqof (lintel, top of the doorframe) become the site of the blood application. The hyssop applicator (Exodus 12:22) and the blood-on-wood pattern anticipate the cross's blood-application to the wooden frame. The verb natan (to give/place) used for putting blood on the doorposts is the same verb used for YHWH's giving of signs and covenants — the blood-sign is an act of covenant protection.
8: kal-maror (bitter herbs): maror (H4844, bitterness) — the bitter herbs of the Passover meal encode the memory of slavery's bitterness (vaymareiru et-chayeihem, 'they made their lives bitter,' Exodus 1:14). Matzot (unleavened bread, H4682): bread made without leaven (chametz), baked in haste. Paul explains the significance in 1 Corinthians 5:7-8: 'chametz' (leaven) = malice and evil; matzot (unleavened bread) = sincerity and truth. The Passover meal contains its own theological interpretation encoded in its ingredients: the bitterness of slavery, the haste of redemption, the purity required for the new beginning.
9: tzali-esh: 'roasted over fire' — not raw (na) and not boiled in water (mevushal bamayim). The specific requirement for fire-roasting distinguishes the Passover preparation from the standard Israelite cooking method (boiling) and from raw consumption. Fire in the OT is associated with divine holiness and judgment (Genesis 3:24; Exodus 3:2; Exodus 19:18). The fire-roasted lamb is thus associated with YHWH's presence and glory. The specific prohibition against boiling may have also distinguished Israel's practice from Egyptian or Canaanite religious cooking customs.
10: lo totiru mimenu ad-boker: 'you shall not leave any of it until morning.' The Passover lamb must be consumed entirely in a single night — nothing stored, nothing saved. The remainder must be burned (ushvarti ba'esh). The prohibition against leftovers has two functions: (1) the lamb is a sacred meal, not ordinary food to be treated casually; (2) the meal commemorates the haste of the exodus — they would leave by morning. The fire-disposal of the remainder also ensures no carcass remains to be desecrated. The totality of consumption pictures the completeness of Christ's atoning work: nothing left incomplete.
11: pesach hu l'YHWH: 'it is the Passover of YHWH.' The noun pesach (H6453) is derived from the verb pasach (Exodus 12:13, 23: 'to pass over/protect'). The traditional interpretation: YHWH “passed over” the blood-marked houses. More nuanced readings suggest pasach means to “protect” or “hover over” (cf. Isaiah 31:5: 'like birds hovering, so YHWH of hosts will protect Jerusalem') rather than merely “skip past.” Bechipazon (in haste, H2649) — the posture of readiness: loins girded, sandals on feet, staff in hand. The Passover meal is eaten as people on the move, not at leisure. The eschatological dimension: the meal anticipates departure from the present age.
12: veavarti beEretz Mitzrayim balaylah hazeh: 'I will pass through Egypt on this night.' YHWH acts in the first person — no angel is mentioned as the agent (contrast the “destroyer” of Exodus 12:23). The Piel of avar (to pass through, cross over) with YHWH as subject is the most direct form of divine personal action in the plague narrative. Ubechol-elohei Mitzrayim e'eseh shefatim: 'against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments.' The plagues are explicitly identified as judgments against Egypt's gods — each plague targets a deity of the Egyptian pantheon: the Nile (Hapi), the sun (Ra/Khepri), life-force (various animal deities). The tenth plague kills the Pharaoh's heir — the incarnation of the divine Horus. YHWH alone is Elohim.
13: vehayah hadam lachem l'ot: 'the blood shall be a sign for you on the houses.' Ot (H226, sign, token) is the same word used for the covenant signs of rainbow (Genesis 9:12-13) and circumcision (Genesis 17:11). The blood-sign on the doorposts is the Passover covenant-sign — the external mark of covenant protection. Ufasachti aleichem: 'I will pass over/protect you' — the verb pasach (H6452) used in the Qal perfect. YHWH sees the blood and acts protectively. The NT parallel: the blood of Christ applied to the believer is the new covenant sign of protection from divine judgment (Romans 3:25: hilasterion; 1 Corinthians 5:7: Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed).
14: zikaron (H2146, memorial, remembrance): 'this day shall be a memorial for you.' The Passover is designed as a zikaron — an act of sacred memory that makes the past event present for each generation. Deuteronomy 16's Passover instructions emphasize zachor (remember) as the primary obligation. The Last Supper's command touto poieite eis ten emen anamnesin ('do this in remembrance of me,' Luke 22:19; 1 Corinthians 11:24-25) uses the Greek anamnesis, the exact LXX translation of zikaron. The Eucharist is the new Passover zikaron — the memorial that makes the new exodus present for the covenant community in each generation.
15: chametz (H2557, leaven, leavened bread): to be removed from houses for seven days. The Feast of Unleavened Bread begins the day after Passover and continues for seven days (Exodus 12:15-20). Chametz is the fermenting agent — it causes bread to rise through a process of decomposition. Paul's interpretation (1 Corinthians 5:6-8) applies chametz as a metaphor for moral corruption: 'a little leaven leavens the whole lump; therefore cleanse out the old leaven that you may be a new lump, as you really are unleavened. For Christ our Passover has been sacrificed.' The seven-day removal of leaven pictures the complete moral renewal that accompanies redemption.
16: miqra-kodesh (H4744, sacred assembly, holy convocation): 'a holy convocation on the first and seventh days.' Miqra is derived from qara (to call, summon) — the assembly is called by YHWH. The sacred assemblies frame the week-long festival. Lo-ta'asu vo melacha ('no work shall be done') — the restriction on labor marks the day as set apart for YHWH's purposes, not human labor. The exception: food preparation (asher ye'achel lechol nefesh) is permitted, grounding the sacred in the physical necessity of eating — the festival is embodied, not merely spiritual.
17: ushemartem et-hamatzot: 'you shall guard the unleavened bread / observe the Festival of Unleavened Bread.' The Qal perfect of shamar (H8104, to keep, guard, observe) — the same verb used for keeping the Sabbath and the covenant. Kiy beetzem hayom hazeh hotzeti et-tzivoteichem: 'on this very day I brought your armies out of Egypt.' Tzivot (armies, hosts) — Israel in the exodus is framed as YHWH's military force. The same language — Tzivot YHWH (the armies of YHWH) — appears in Exodus 12:41. The exodus is not a refugee escape but a military march of YHWH's forces.
18: beavirim ad-esrim veechad lachodesh: 'from the evening of the fourteenth to the twenty-first.' The Feast of Unleavened Bread begins on the evening of the fourteenth (when the Passover lamb is eaten) and continues until the twenty-first — seven full days. The precision of the calendar dates reflects the covenantal seriousness: the times are YHWH's appointed times (moedim), not flexible traditions. Erev ad-erev (evening to evening) reflects the Hebrew reckoning of days from sunset to sunset (Genesis 1:5 pattern), the same principle underlying the Sabbath.
19: kol-se'or lo yimmatze bevatechem: 'no leaven shall be found in your houses' — the command escalates from removing leaven (Exodus 12:15) to ensuring none is even present. The thoroughness of the pre-Passover leaven search (bedikat chametz) became one of Judaism's most detailed ritual observances. Ger u-vezrach ha'aretz ('both for the foreigner and for the native of the land'): the rule applies equally to resident aliens and native Israelites. The equal application to gerim (resident foreigners) anticipates the NT abolition of ethnic distinctions within the covenant community.
20: kol-machmat lo tochelu: 'eat nothing leavened.' The prohibition is absolute and repeated for emphasis throughout the chapter. The seven-day period of unleavened bread was so central to Israelite identity that chametz became a theological symbol. The Qumran community's strict unleavened-bread practice and Paul's application to moral purity (1 Corinthians 5:7-8) both reflect the deep symbolic resonance of the chametz/matzot opposition — corruption vs. purity, old age vs. new creation.
21: mishchu uqechu lachem seh lemishpechotechem: 'draw/select and take a lamb for your families.' Moses now communicates YHWH's instructions to the elders of Israel — the chain of authority flows: YHWH → Moses → elders → people. The Qal imperative mishchu (draw out, select) specifically means to draw/choose from the flock — a deliberate act of selection. The communal family-centered instruction reinforces the household structure of the Passover. Moses instructs Israel to “slaughter the Passover lamb” (vesachatu hapasach) — the lamb's slaughter is identified with the feast itself.
22: ezov (H231, hyssop): a small bushy plant with absorbent properties used in purification rituals throughout the OT (Leviticus 14:4-6, 49-52; Numbers 19:6, 18; Psalms 51:7). The hyssop branch dipped in the basin of blood and applied to the doorframes is the original ritual use of this plant. Its later purification associations (Psalms 51:7: 'purge me with hyssop and I shall be clean') ground the Passover blood-application in the broader purity theology. John 19:29 places a hyssop branch in the scene of Jesus's crucifixion — a deliberate echo of the Passover hyssop application.
23: hamashchit (H4889, the destroyer): 'YHWH will not allow the destroyer to enter your houses.' The mashchit (destroyer, from shachat, to destroy, corrupt) appears here as the agent of the final plague — distinct from YHWH himself acting directly (Exodus 12:12). Hebrews 11:28 summarizes: 'By faith he kept the Passover and sprinkled the blood, so that the Destroyer of the firstborn might not touch them.' The mashchit is the same figure as the 'destroying angel' in later texts (2 Samuel 24:16; 1 Chronicles 21:15). The blood on the doorpost stops the destroyer — it is the visible covenant sign that marks the household as YHWH's.
24: ushemertem et-hadavar hazeh lechok lecha ulvaneicha ad-olam: 'keep this word as a statute for you and your children forever.' The Passover is not merely historical commemoration but perpetual covenant obligation — lechok olam (an eternal statute). The generational instruction follows in Exodus 12:26-27: the children's question and the parent's answer are built into the ritual, ensuring the narrative is transmitted. The question-and-answer structure of the Passover became the template for catechetical instruction throughout Israel's covenant history.
25: vahayah ki tavo'u el-ha'aretz: 'when you enter the land.' The Passover instruction looks forward to the land — it is given in Egypt but oriented toward Canaan. The future tense (tavo'u) frames the current captivity within a trajectory of promise and fulfillment. Ushmartem et-ha'avoda hazot ('you shall keep this service/worship') — the word avoda (H5656, service, work, worship) was used for Israel's slavery in Egypt and is now redirected: the same word describes Israel's worship-service to YHWH. Redemption from slavery transforms avoda from oppression to devotion.
26: vehayah ki yomeru aleichem bneichem: 'when your children say to you...' The intergenerational transmission of the Passover narrative is built into the covenant structure: children will ask, parents will answer. The Haggadah's four sons (the wise, the wicked, the simple, and the one who does not know how to ask) are rabbinic elaborations of this built-in catechetical question. The Passover's narrative structure — question, answer, story — becomes the template for Christian kerygmatic preaching: the gospel is proclaimed in response to questions generated by covenant signs (baptism, Eucharist).
27: zevach pesach hu l'YHWH: 'it is the Passover sacrifice to YHWH.' The answer to the children's question names the meal as a zevach (sacrifice, offering) — the Passover is not merely a family dinner but a sacrificial act. Asher pasach al-batei vnei Yisrael beMitzrayim ('who passed over the houses of Israel in Egypt') — the explanation links the meal to the original event it commemorates. Vayiqod ha'am vayishtachavu ('the people bowed down and worshiped') — the response to the instruction is worship, not merely compliance. The Passover narrative evokes awe and gratitude before it demands observance.
28: vayelechu vaya'asu benei Yisrael ka'asher tzivah YHWH: 'the Israelites went and did exactly as YHWH had commanded.' The compliance formula (ka'asher tzivah YHWH, 'as YHWH commanded') appears repeatedly throughout Exodus (cf. Exodus 39-40 re. the tabernacle). The formula marks covenant faithfulness: Israel's obedience to the precise Passover instructions is the covenant act that activates the divine protection. The collective obedience of the community (benei Yisrael, sons of Israel) stands as a model of covenant response — hearing and doing.
29: vayehiy bechatzot halaylah: 'at midnight' — the fulfillment of Moses's prophetic announcement (Exodus 11:4). The precision of the fulfillment — at the exact time YHWH announced — validates the prophetic word. Vayak YHWH kol-bechor beeretz Mitzrayim: 'YHWH struck every firstborn in Egypt' — the Hiphil of nakah (H5221, to strike, smite) with YHWH as subject. The tenth plague requires no naturalistic explanation: it is direct divine action at the announced time. The scope — from Pharaoh's throne to the prisoner in the dungeon to the livestock — demonstrates YHWH's sovereignty over every social stratum of Egypt.
30: vataqom hu vechol-avadav vechol-Mitzrayim laylah: 'he arose in the night, he and all his officials and all Egypt.' The national night-rising echoes the earlier plagues (Exodus 8:20: Pharaoh arose early) but this time there is no negotiation — only tzeaqah gedolah (great cry). Ki-ein bayit asher ein-sham met: 'for there was not a house where there was no one dead' — the totality of the judgment is emphasized. The Egyptian household cry mirrors Israel's cry under slavery (Exodus 2:23; Exodus 3:7). YHWH has heard the cry of his people and now Egypt experiences the cry of the bereaved.
31: qumu tze'u mitoch ammi: 'rise up, go out from among my people.' Pharaoh's night summons to Moses and Aaron ends with what he refused for ten plagues: 'go, serve YHWH as you have said.' Gam-tzoneichem gam-biqrechem qechu ka'asher dibartem: 'even your flocks and your herds, take as you have spoken' — every condition Moses demanded is conceded in a single sentence. Uvelachtem gam oti: 'and bless me also' — Pharaoh now asks a blessing from the man he commanded to leave his presence never to return (Exodus 10:28). The reversal is complete: the oppressor becomes the petitioner.
32: uvelachtem gam oti: 'bless me also.' Pharaoh's request for blessing from Moses — whom he had threatened with death on sight (Exodus 10:28) — is the ultimate reversal of the power dynamic. Varets vatekeved al-ha'am ('the Egyptians urged the people, pressing them urgently') — the very Egyptians who enslaved Israel now cannot expel them fast enough. The verb chazaq (to be strong, urge strongly) used of the Egyptians' urging echoes the chazaq of Pharaoh's hardened heart — the strength that held Israel captive is now directed toward releasing them.
33: vatechez Mitzrayim al-ha'am lemahher leshalcham min-ha'aretz: 'Egypt urged the people to hurry, to send them out of the land.' Ki amru kulanu meitim: 'for they said, “we are all dying.”' The Egyptians' fear of total annihilation drives the urgency. The reversal is stark: Israel came to Egypt as seventy people fleeing famine (Genesis 46:27); they leave as a nation of 600,000 men (Exodus 12:37), bearing Egypt's wealth (Exodus 12:35-36), with Egypt itself begging them to leave. The Exodus narrative's dramatic reversal — from humiliation to exaltation, from few to many, from poor to wealthy — is a pattern that the NT applies to Christ's death and resurrection.
34: vayisa ha'am et-beseto: 'the people carried their dough on their shoulders.' The kneading troughs (mishtarotam) were wrapped in clothing and carried — because they could not delay to leaven the bread. This is the factual origin of the matzot that would be baked at Sukkoth: the unleavened bread is not merely commanded but physically produced by the urgency of the departure. The hasty exit embeds the theology into the material circumstance: redemption comes without delay and cannot wait for the ordinary processes of life to complete themselves.
35: uvenei Yisrael asu kidvar Moshe vayish'alu meiMitzrayim: 'the Israelites did as Moses instructed and asked of the Egyptians.' The plundering of Egypt fulfills YHWH's promise to Abraham in Genesis 15:14: 'afterward they shall come out with great possessions (rechush gadol).' Israel takes keli-keseph u-chelei zahav u-semalot (articles of silver, gold, and clothing) — the material wealth of the ancient world's most prosperous nation. Paul interprets this in the Spirit: the spoiling of Egypt is a type of all truth belonging to the church and being reclaimed from the “Egypt” of pagan culture (the Alexandrian tradition of theological use of pagan philosophy drew on this).
36: vaYHWH natan et-chen ha'am beynei Mitzrayim vayash'ilum: 'YHWH gave the people favor in the eyes of Egypt, and they gave them what they asked.' The chen YHWH sovereignly provided (cf. Exodus 11:3) was the instrument of the plundering. Vayenatzelu et-Mitzrayim: 'they plundered Egypt' — the Piel of natsal (to strip, rescue) used in a plundering context. The same verb is used in 3:22 and Exodus 12:35 — YHWH promised the spoiling, Moses announced it, and now it occurs. The plundering of Egypt is the material accompaniment of the spiritual liberation: the enslaved gain not only freedom but the material means to build the tabernacle in the wilderness.
37: shesh meot elef ragley hagevarin: 'about 600,000 men on foot.' The number has generated enormous scholarly discussion: if 600,000 men (adult males), the total population including women and children would be 2-3 million — an enormous number for the Sinai wilderness. Some scholars read eleph (H505) not as 1,000 but as a military unit of variable size (suggesting ~5,550-6,000 men total), following George Mendenhall's analysis. The narrative's point is clear regardless of the exact count: Israel left Egypt as a nation, not a family. The 600,000 figure also connects to the census in Numbers 1-2 (603,550 men), creating a coherent numerology for the wilderness generation.
38: veGam erev rav ala itam: 'a mixed multitude also went up with them.' The erev rav (mixed multitude, H6154) accompanying Israel is the first indication that the Exodus was not ethnically exclusive: non-Israelites joined the departure. The same group is later blamed for the craving of meat (Numbers 11:4: ve'asafsuf asher beqirbo). The erev rav may have included other Semitic slaves, Egyptians married to Israelites, or people drawn by YHWH's power. The principle of the mixed multitude joining Israel prefigures Ruth, Rahab, and the Gentile inclusion in the covenant community.
39: ki lo chametz ki-gerushim miMitzrayim velo yachlu lehitmahmeah: 'for it was not leavened because they were expelled from Egypt and could not delay.' The physical reason for the unleavened bread is provided: there was no time to leaven it. The haste of the departure is the empirical origin of the theological requirement. The connection between urgency and unleavened bread is made explicit: the matzot that Israel eats at Passover every year is the bread of urgent departure — the bread of those who cannot linger in the old life because the new creation calls them forward.
40: umoshav benei Yisrael asher yashvu beMitzrayim shloshim shanah ve'arba meot shanah: '430 years.' The 430-year sojourn in Egypt (from the LXX version, extended to include time in Canaan: 'the sojourn ... in Egypt and Canaan') is cited by Paul in Galatians 3:17: 'the law, which came 430 years afterward, does not annul a covenant previously ratified by God.' Paul's use confirms the 430-year figure and connects it to the Abrahamic covenant timeline: the Sinai law came 430 years after Abraham, not 430 years after the entry to Egypt (supporting the LXX's longer reckoning). The precision of the period — vayehiy mikets shloshim shanah ve'arba meot shanah (at the end of 430 years, to the very day) — emphasizes YHWH's absolute control over the timing of the Exodus.
41: tzivot YHWH (armies of YHWH): Israel leaves Egypt not as a refugee crowd but as tzivot YHWH — YHWH's military forces. The same term (tzva'ot, hosts/armies) is the root of the divine name YHWH Tzvaot (YHWH of Hosts/Armies), one of the most frequent divine names in the OT prophets. The exodus is a military event: YHWH is the commander, Israel is the army, and the exit from Egypt is a march. This militarized framing of the Exodus is the background for Ephesians 6's armor of God — the Christian life is framed within the same exodus-march-warfare structure.
42: leil shimmurim hu l'YHWH: 'a night of keeping/vigil for YHWH' — shimmurim (H8107, from shamar, to keep, guard) describes the night as one of active divine vigilance. YHWH keeps watch over his people on this night of all nights — the very night he acts to deliver them. The double use of shimmurim ('a night of vigil for YHWH to bring them out ... this same night is a night of vigil for YHWH for all the Israelites throughout their generations') frames both the original event and its annual commemoration as acts of reciprocal watching: YHWH watches over Israel, Israel watches through the night. The NT's night-vigil eschatology (Matthew 25:13: keep watch; Luke 12:37: blessed are those servants whom the master finds awake) draws on the leil shimmurim pattern.
43: zot chuqat hapesach: 'this is the regulation of the Passover.' The Passover regulations (Exodus 12:43-50) address participation eligibility: the central criterion is circumcision (mila). Kol-ben-nechar lo-yochal bo: 'no foreigner (lit. son of a foreign people) shall eat of it.' The restriction is covenant-based, not ethnic: the ben-nechar is distinguished from the ger (resident alien, Exodus 12:48) who may eat if circumcised. The circumcision requirement makes the covenant sign the entry point for Passover participation — covenant membership precedes covenant meal.
44: ve'eved ish miqnat-keseph: 'a slave purchased with money' — a permanent household slave (eved) who has been circumcised may eat the Passover. The contrast with the hired worker (toshav ve'sachir, Exodus 12:45) who may not eat reflects the distinction between full household membership (the slave belongs to the household permanently) and transient engagement. The slave's circumcision integrates him into the covenant community. Paul's use of slave imagery for the Christian's relationship to Christ (Romans 6:18-22; 1 Corinthians 7:22) draws on this covenant-household logic: belonging to Christ means full participation in the covenant meal.
45: toshav vesachir lo-yochal bo: 'a temporary resident and a hired worker may not eat of it.' The toshav (sojourner, resident alien without permanent status) and the sachir (hired worker, day laborer) are excluded — their relationship to the household is temporary and transactional, not covenantal. This verse creates the distinction that Exodus 12:48's exception clarifies: a resident foreigner who submits to circumcision transitions from toshav to full covenant member. The line between inclusion and exclusion is the covenant sign, not ethnicity.
46: veetzem lo tishberu-bo: 'you shall not break a bone of it.' The prohibition against breaking the Passover lamb's bones (also in Numbers 9:12) is cited in John 19:36 as fulfilled when the soldiers did not break Jesus's legs: 'these things took place that the Scripture might be fulfilled: not one of his bones will be broken.' The unbroken-bone requirement for the Passover lamb is one of the most precise typological fulfillments in the NT. The physical integrity of the lamb corresponds to the physical integrity of Christ's body — the true Passover lamb. Psalms 34:20 ('he keeps all his bones; not one of them is broken') applies the bone-protection language to the righteous man, which John also sees fulfilled in Jesus.
47: kol-adat Yisrael ya'asu oto: 'the whole congregation of Israel shall keep it.' The Passover is a community-wide obligation — not optional, not individual. The edah (congregation, assembly) is the technical term for the covenant community gathered. The communal nature of the Passover meal is its fundamental character: no individual participates alone; the whole covenant community celebrates together. This community-wide structure is the background for Paul's 'the cup of blessing that we bless, is it not a participation in the blood of Christ? The bread that we break, is it not a participation in the body of Christ? Because there is one bread, we who are many are one body' (1 Corinthians 10:16-17).
48: vechi-yagur itcha ger ve'asa pesach l'YHWH himol lo chol-zachur: 'if a stranger dwells with you and wants to keep the Passover to YHWH, he must have all the males circumcised.' The inclusion of the circumcised foreigner (ger) in the Passover community is the proto-Gentile inclusion: the covenant meal is open to those who receive the covenant sign. This verse becomes a NT proof-text for Gentile inclusion: Paul argues in Galatians 3:8 that Abraham's covenant was always designed to include the nations, and the circumcision requirement has been replaced by faith in Christ (Galatians 3:26-29: 'in Christ Jesus you are all sons of God through faith ... neither Jew nor Greek').
49: torah achat yihyeh laezrach velager: 'the same law shall apply to the native and to the stranger.' The principle of equal legal standing for the native and the resident alien is stated explicitly — one law (torah achat), not a two-tier system. This principle appears repeatedly in the Torah (Leviticus 24:22; Numbers 9:14; Exodus 15:15-16) and is foundational to the NT abolition of Jewish-Gentile distinction in the church: 'for there is no distinction between Jew and Greek' (Romans 10:12); 'there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28).
50: vaya'asu kol-benei Yisrael ka'asher tzivah YHWH: 'all the Israelites did just as YHWH had commanded.' The compliance formula closes the Passover instruction section. The emphasis on full obedience (kol-benei Yisrael, all Israel) and precision (ka'asher tzivah YHWH, exactly as YHWH commanded) frames the Exodus as a covenant-keeping event: Israel's obedience to the Passover instructions is the faith-act that receives YHWH's protection. The same pattern — hearing the word, doing it precisely — underlies the NT's 'faith that works' (James 2:17-18) and Jesus's parable of the two builders (Matthew 7:24-27).
51: vayehiy betzem hayom hazeh hotzi YHWH et-benei Yisrael mei'eretz Mitzrayim al-tzivotam: 'on that very day YHWH brought the Israelites out of Egypt by their divisions.' Betzem hayom hazeh ('on this very day') — the phrase used throughout the Exodus for the precision of divine timing (also in Exodus 12:17, 41). The book of Exodus closes its narrative of the departure with this summary: YHWH is the subject, Israel is the object, and the movement is out from Egypt. The phrase al-tzivotam ('by their divisions/armies') repeats the military framing of Exodus 12:41 — Israel leaves as YHWH's organized military force, not as a chaotic mass. The Exodus is a completed, organized, divinely orchestrated event.
